title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — July 14, 2026
headline: A UN mission formally finds Sudan's El Fasher atrocities bear 'distinct markers of genocide' -- a score already at zero cannot move lower.
summary: A UN Fact-Finding Mission formally found that Rapid Support Forces atrocities in El Fasher, Sudan bear 'distinct markers of genocide' and opened an urgent new inquiry into a similar siege forming around El Obeid. Sudan's score was already at zero out of 100, the lowest possible mark, so this grave finding cannot lower it further -- it is documented as a major evidentiary milestone instead. Tunisia's proposed score cut, from 34.4 to 23.8 out of 100, was reaffirmed for a fifth straight day with no new escalation, so the published score stays at 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding. Iran's own conduct -- political executions at a multi-decade high -- intensifies an already-documented pattern, but Iran's score of 2.5 out of 100 sits so close to zero that even a move to the floor falls short of the five-point shift needed to change a published score. Separately, the United States resumed a naval blockade in the Strait of Hormuz on July 14 and briefly proposed, then reversed, a new shipping fee; that action is scored against the United States, not Iran. Venezuela's earthquake death toll held flat at 4,490, three weeks after the quake, with the increase reflecting the disaster's scale rather than a new government failure, so its score holds at 18 of 100. Israel, Russia, and Yemen all remain at the lowest possible score, 0 of 100, as fresh evidence of harm continues to arrive in each country. Anthropic holds at 59.1 of 100 with no new trigger this cycle. A long-running question about whether Mali and Burkina Faso are scored consistently for similar conduct is now open about twenty-one days.

pipeline.tunisiaHandling: Tunisia's proposed score of 23.8 of 100 (down from a published 34.4) remains a proposal only, reaffirmed for a fifth cycle with no new dated escalation beyond the already-reflected mass-sentencing record. The published score stays at 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding. No change has been made to the live index.

topSignals.title: A UN Mission Formally Finds Sudan's El Fasher Killings Bear 'Distinct Markers of Genocide'
topSignals.whyItMatters: The UN's most serious possible legal characterization of mass violence now applies to Sudan, but the country's score was already at zero, the lowest mark the benchmark gives.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds a published score of 0 out of 100, the lowest score in the benchmark's Critical band. On July 8-9, the UN Human Rights Council's Independent Fact-Finding Mission for Sudan formally reported that mass killings, abductions, and gang rape carried out by the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) in El Fasher -- where more than 6,000 people were killed in three days as the city fell in October 2025 -- bear 'distinct markers of genocide.' The Mission also opened an urgent new inquiry into a strikingly similar pattern now forming around the city of El Obeid: encirclement, strikes on infrastructure, and blocked access to food, water, and medicine. The RSF is a non-state armed group, so this finding is attributed to the RSF's own conduct rather than Sudan's government. But Sudan's zero score independently reflects the Sudanese state's own conduct, including obstruction of humanitarian access during the same civil war. Because the score is already at the lowest possible mark, no evidence -- however grave -- can lower it further. The benchmark records this as a major evidentiary milestone rather than treating the arithmetic ceiling as indifference to the finding. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: A UN Fact-Finding Mission formally characterized RSF mass killings, abductions, and gang rape in El Fasher as bearing markers of genocide.
The UN probe found RSF mass killings and gang rapes in El Fasher amount to genocide and warned the same tactics are now being deployed around El Obeid.

topSignals.title: Tunisia's Proposed Score Cut Is Reaffirmed for a Fifth Day With No New Escalation
topSignals.whyItMatters: A proposed score cut has now sat unresolved for five nights running with fully consistent evidence, but the published score does not move until the benchmark's editors act on it.
topSignals.description: Tunisia holds a published score of 34.4 out of 100, in the Developing band. A review proposed on July 10 suggested a lower score of 23.8 out of 100, based on the 25-year prison sentence given to Sihem Bensedrine, the former head of Tunisia's own Truth and Dignity Commission, and the mass sentencing of 21 opposition figures to 12-to-35-year prison terms on July 7-8. Tonight, no new dated escalation surfaced beyond that already-reflected record; the 62nd session of the UN Human Rights Council, which closed July 8 with a statement of deep concern, remains the most recent institutional signal. The proposed figure of 23.8 is unchanged, and no second, duplicate proposal was opened. The published score of 34.4 stays in place while the benchmark's editors review the proposal for a fifth consecutive night. Confidence: medium.

evidence.quote: Former truth commission president Sihem Bensedrine sentenced to 25 years in prison
evidence.claim: Tunisia is imprisoning the leader of its own past accountability body, reversing transitional justice.

topSignals.title: Iran's Near-Zero Score Cannot Fall Further as the US Resumes a Hormuz Naval Blockade
topSignals.whyItMatters: Iran's own record of executions is intensifying, but the score is already so close to zero that even severe new evidence cannot register as a change under the benchmark's own math.
topSignals.description: Iran holds a published score of 2.5 out of 100, in the Critical band. Political executions and repression continue at a multi-decade high -- more than 784 so far this year -- deepening a pattern already counted against Iran's score. But 2.5 points is nearly the entire distance left to the lowest possible score, so even a move all the way to zero falls short of the five-point shift the benchmark requires before changing a published number. Separately, the United States and Iran exchanged fire for a third consecutive weekend, and US Central Command resumed a naval blockade of ships entering and leaving Iranian ports starting 4pm on July 14. President Trump also proposed, then reversed within the same day, a 20 percent fee on cargo shipped through the Strait of Hormuz. That action is scored against the United States' own conduct, not against Iran, and the United States was not among tonight's fifteen assessed countries. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: US Central Command said it would resume the naval blockade of ships entering or leaving Iranian ports from 4pm on July 14, as the US and Iran exchanged fire for a third consecutive weekend.
President Trump proposed a 20 percent cargo-value fee on Hormuz shippers, then reversed course the same day, July 14, saying Gulf-state investment would replace that revenue instead.

topSignals.title: Venezuela's Earthquake Death Toll Holds at 4,490 Three Weeks In, Still Short of the Score-Change Line
topSignals.whyItMatters: A death toll from a natural disaster that stops climbing this week is not, by itself, evidence of government misconduct, so Venezuela's score stays the same.
topSignals.description: Venezuela holds a published score of 18 out of 100, in the Critical band. Twenty-one days after the earthquake, the government's official toll held flat at 4,490 dead, with 16,740 injured and more than 19,500 people still living in displacement camps. The benchmark treats the disaster's scale, not a new government failure, as the driver of these numbers. No documented aid diversion or denial of responder access was found this cycle. The evidenced decline in Venezuela's own-conduct response score stays in the low negative 3-point range, short of the 5-point threshold the benchmark requires. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Venezuela's official earthquake death toll held at 4,490 as of July 13, with 16,740 injured and no estimate given for the number still unaccounted for.

topSignals.title: Israel, Russia, and Yemen All Hold at Zero as Fresh Harm Keeps Arriving
topSignals.whyItMatters: All three countries already sit at the lowest possible score. Tonight's evidence cannot lower any of them further, but each event reinforces why the score sits at the floor.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at 0 of 100. A July 14 Israeli airstrike on a police post in Jabalia killed at least seven people, including the head of the local police force; separate strikes and gunfire killed a 10-year-old boy in Rafah and a 36-year-old man in Khan Younis. The cumulative toll since the ceasefire began has passed 1,100 killed, out of more than 3,689 reported ceasefire violations. Russia holds at 0 of 100, where the same missile and drone campaign against Ukraine remains the operative record, alongside the European Union's earlier sanctions on six individuals over chemical-weapons development tied to the 2024 death of opposition figure Alexei Navalny. Yemen holds at 0 of 100, where the war's largest planned prisoner exchange, involving roughly 1,728 detainees, remains stalled; a UN envoy's July 11 statement that both sides renewed their commitment has not yet turned into an actual exchange. None of the three scores can move lower, since all are already at the floor. Confidence: high for all three.

evidence.claim: An Israeli airstrike on a Hamas-led police post in Jabalia killed at least seven people, including the head of the Jabalia police force; separately, 10-year-old Motaz Abu Shaar was killed by Israeli fire in Rafah.

topSignals.title: An Ebola Outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo Jumps From 600 to 702 Deaths in Two Days
topSignals.whyItMatters: One of Africa's fastest-growing Ebola outbreaks just accelerated sharply, but the Democratic Republic of the Congo's (DRC) score is already so close to zero that the jump cannot register as a change.
topSignals.description: The Democratic Republic of the Congo holds a published score of 2.3 out of 100, in the Critical band. Its Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak reached 702 confirmed deaths and 1,926 confirmed cases as of July 11-12, up from 600 deaths and 1,759 cases just two days earlier -- a pace Africa's public-health agency calls the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak on the continent. The World Health Organization now estimates the true death toll could run as high as 4,000 to 8,000. The benchmark treats the epidemic itself as a natural event, not government conduct, while a health-worker wage dispute and disrupted access in the hardest-hit province are already factored into the near-floor score as aggravating factors. Because the score sits only 2.3 points above zero, no evidence this severe can register as a five-point change. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: The DRC's Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak reached at least 702 confirmed deaths and 1,926 confirmed cases as of July 11-12, up from 600-625 deaths two days earlier, described by Africa CDC as the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak on the continent.

topSignals.title: The Mali-Burkina Faso Scoring Question Reaches Roughly Twenty-One Days Open
topSignals.whyItMatters: A long-running question about whether two neighboring countries are scored consistently for similar conduct still has no answer, now past three weeks unresolved.
topSignals.description: Mali holds at 12.5 out of 100, in the Critical band. Tonight, Mali confirmed again as a fuel and food blockade by the armed group JNIM continued to cripple the capital, Bamako, and the government said it broke a separate rebel blockade around a northern army base. The dominant source of harm remains a non-state armed group, not the government's own conduct, so Mali's score does not move. The open question is different: whether Mali's score of 12.5 is fairly calibrated against Burkina Faso's 6.3, given that both governments face comparable armed-group violence and comparable documented abuses by their own forces. That question has now gone without a decision for roughly twenty-one consecutive days, the benchmark's second-longest open item after Tunisia's pending proposal, and it remains a matter for the benchmark's coordinating team rather than a single night's assessment. Burkina Faso itself was not assessed tonight. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Mali's military said it broke a rebel blockade around a strategic northern army base, following an offensive by an allied Tuareg armed group.

topSignals.title: Anthropic Holds at 59.1 With No New Trigger as the China Backdoor Allegation Stays Held
topSignals.whyItMatters: A rival government's allegation from earlier this month still does not clear the benchmark's bar for a score change, and no new development arrived tonight to reopen the question.
topSignals.description: Anthropic holds a published score of 59.1 out of 100, in the Functional band. The Chinese government's allegation that Claude Code contains a hidden backdoor -- tested against the benchmark's conversion-trigger rule and held on July 12 -- remains the operative record; no new independently-corroborated episode arrived this cycle. A new privacy-policy provision that lets Anthropic request age or identity verification, effective July 8, is read as a protective change, not a negative one. The published score holds at 59.1, with the heightened downward watch set on July 12 remaining in place. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Anthropic continues to reject China's backdoor allegation, characterizing the mechanism as an anti-distillation experiment already removed in a July 1 release, before the Chinese alert surfaced.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Due-process restoration, civil-society reinstatement, and reversal of the indefinite re-election amendment remain the path back toward 20 of 100. The 2027 election under the amendment is the next scheduled test.
boundaryWatch.note: El Salvador sits at 15.0 of 100 in the Critical band, 5.0 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad -- is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. Hong Kong issued arrest warrants and bounties on 15 more overseas activists and tightened Prison Rules governing inmate-lawyer contact; no newly and reliably dated escalation beyond the already-confirmed record.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A second undisclosed telemetry episode, evidence the removed tracker was broader or retained longer than stated, or a regulator or court finding of deceptive practice would each independently move the score toward a change. The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. No new conversion-trigger event arrived tonight; a new age/identity-verification privacy provision, effective July 8, is read as protective. The heightened watch set July 12 remains in place.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. A fresh Israeli drone strike killed four, including a school principal, in Nabatieh al-Fawqa despite the June 21 ceasefire -- Israel's conduct, not Lebanon's own.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently verified deliberate targeting of civilians in a cross-border strike would move Pakistan's score down; durable relief for detained protesters and disappeared Balochistan residents would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. A Balochistan security operation now reports a combined 125 alleged militants killed as of July 14, atop the already-priced enforced-disappearance pattern; the figure is security-source-reported and not independently verified.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent confirmation of a discrete own-conduct failure, such as documented aid diversion or denial of responder access, is the next event that would move Venezuela's score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Venezuela sits at 18.0 of 100. The earthquake death toll held flat at 4,490 three weeks in; the evidenced decline remains in the low negative 3-point range, short of the 5-point threshold.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration in Borno state combined with documented aid obstruction is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Nigeria sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points below the Developing band. The World Food Programme's worst-in-a-decade hunger record remains operative, continuing the already-priced June 19 downgrade.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. The Ebola outbreak jumped from 600 to 702 deaths in two days, the fastest-growing on the continent per Africa CDC.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented expulsions of Bengali Muslim and Rohingya populations, and continued digital surveillance targeting minority groups, remain under watch. Not assessed tonight.
boundaryWatch.note: India sits at 15.6 of 100, 0.6 points above the absolute floor. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa, combined with documented government aid obstruction, is the next scored trigger. The famine-risk window runs through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime would move Kuwait's score down; durable expansion of bidoon rights would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. A fuel and food blockade by the armed group JNIM continues to cripple Bamako. The calibration question against Burkina Faso has now been open roughly twenty-one consecutive days, with no resolution timeline set.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Burkina Faso's conduct against Mali's (12.5 of 100) is the next scored event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Burkina Faso sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight. The calibration question against Mali remains open roughly twenty-one consecutive days.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Bolivia's conduct profile, as an elected government under economic and civil-unrest strain, against Critical-band peers facing state collapse or mass atrocity is the next event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Bolivia sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight; the calibration question against Burkina Faso and Somalia remains open.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The outcome of the Justice Department's False Claims Act complaint or the Barrows v. Humana class action over AI-driven claim denials would confirm the current Developing-band placement either way.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana sits at 35.2 of 100, 4.8 points below the Functional band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Forced cuts that measurably reduce student or staff support would count against the score; abandoning its academic-freedom stance would as well.
boundaryWatch.note: Princeton sits at 57.8 of 100, 2.2 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented atrocities by Haitian state security forces, or deliberate relief obstruction, would move the score down. Verified state-led civilian protection would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Haiti sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A discrete new harm category beyond documented opposition and press suppression is the next event that would move Turkey's score further down.
boundaryWatch.note: Turkey sits at 10.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.note: Kenya sits at 35.9 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented own-conduct deterioration in epidemic response, such as government obstruction of WHO response corridors, would be the next event that could push Uganda back into the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Uganda sits at 20.3 of 100, just 0.3 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A merits ruling or verdict for the states, a material settlement, or new dated evidence of youth-safety harm or internal-findings suppression is the next event that would move Meta's score.
boundaryWatch.note: Meta Platforms sits at 7.8 of 100, near the bottom of the Fortune 500 list. Not assessed tonight.

emergingRisks.risk: A UN mission's formal genocide-markers finding for El Fasher and a new inquiry into a replicating El Obeid siege pattern set a precedent for how the benchmark documents grave findings that arrive at an already-floored score.
emergingRisks.detail: Sudan's score cannot move below zero, but the Fact-Finding Mission's new El Obeid inquiry is worth tracking closely, since the pattern mirrors El Fasher's trajectory before the city fell.

emergingRisks.risk: Tunisia's pending score-review proposal has now gone five full cycles without a decision from the benchmark's editors, even as the underlying evidence stays fully consistent.
emergingRisks.detail: The proposed 23.8 of 100 stays within the Developing band and is not yet applied to the published record. A fifth consecutive cycle without action keeps the published score at a value the evidence no longer fully supports.

emergingRisks.risk: A resumed US naval blockade in the Strait of Hormuz is a concrete escalation in United States conduct that is not yet reflected in any country's own score this cycle.
emergingRisks.detail: The United States' Critical-band composite (17.5) was not reassessed tonight even as CENTCOM moved from threats to an active blockade and a same-day proposed-then-reversed cargo fee.

emergingRisks.risk: The Mali-Burkina Faso scoring-consistency question is now open roughly twenty-one consecutive days, the benchmark's second-longest unresolved item after Tunisia.
emergingRisks.detail: Mali confirmed again tonight with a non-state armed group as the dominant source of harm, leaving the underlying calibration question about the two countries' government-conduct scores unresolved.

emergingRisks.risk: An Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo accelerated sharply this window, though the near-floor score cannot register it as a change yet.
emergingRisks.detail: The death toll jumped from 600 to 702 in two days, the fastest pace yet in an outbreak already described as the fastest-growing on the continent.

emergingRisks.risk: A cluster of near-zero scores across six countries means severe new evidence in any of them can no longer register as a public score change.
emergingRisks.detail: Six of tonight's fifteen assessed entities are at or within roughly 2.5 points of the absolute floor, illustrating how the benchmark's near-floor arithmetic can outpace even severe escalations, as tonight's Sudan and DRC findings both show.

methodologyNotes.name: A Grave Finding Can Reinforce a Floor Without Changing It
methodologyNotes.description: When new evidence of extreme severity arrives for an entity already at the benchmark's lowest possible score, the score cannot move lower -- there is no room left below zero. Tonight, a UN mission's formal finding that Sudan's El Fasher killings bear 'distinct markers of genocide' reinforces Sudan's already-floored score rather than producing a numeric change. The benchmark records this as a significant evidentiary milestone, not a null result.

methodologyNotes.name: A Pending Proposal Can Be Reaffirmed Without Changing Its Number
methodologyNotes.description: When the evidence behind a score-change proposal stays consistent night after night, the benchmark records that the finding was reaffirmed rather than opening a new, overlapping proposal. Tonight, no new escalation surfaced beyond Tunisia's already-reflected mass-sentencing record for a fifth consecutive cycle. The proposed score of 23.8 out of 100 stayed the same; the published score remains 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding.

methodologyNotes.name: A Score Near Zero Has Little Room Left to Fall
methodologyNotes.description: Iran's score of 2.5 out of 100 is so close to the lowest possible mark that even a severe increase in documented executions cannot produce the 5-point change the benchmark requires before adjusting a published score. The Democratic Republic of the Congo (2.3) sits in the same position, and its Ebola death toll jumped from 600 to 702 in just two days this cycle without crossing the threshold. The benchmark records these as near-floor confirmations rather than treating the arithmetic limit as evidence that the underlying conduct does not matter.

methodologyNotes.name: Interstate Harm Is Scored Against the Actor, Not the Target
methodologyNotes.description: When one country's military acts against another country, the benchmark scores that action against the acting country's own conduct, not against the country it targets. Tonight, the US Central Command's resumed naval blockade of Iranian ports is logged as United States conduct, not scored against Iran's own record.

methodologyNotes.name: A Natural Disaster's Toll Is Not, by Itself, New Misconduct
methodologyNotes.description: When a death toll from an earthquake, flood, or similar disaster stops climbing or continues to rise, the benchmark separates deaths caused by the disaster's scale from deaths caused by a government's own failure to respond. Venezuela's earthquake toll held flat at 4,490 three weeks after the quake, with no documented own-conduct failure, so the score held.

forwardTriggers.trigger: The natural seasonal peak for the current Ebola outbreak closes. Documented government obstruction of WHO response corridors is the trigger toward the absolute floor.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. A second undisclosed telemetry episode or a regulator finding of deceptive practice would be the next event that could move the score.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. No public compliance roadmap has been published yet.
The famine-risk window closes. A formal famine declaration combined with documented government aid obstruction would move the score.
The 2027 election under the July 2025 indefinite re-election amendment. Restoring due process and reinstating civil society are the conditions that would move the score back up.
The outcome of the UN Fact-Finding Mission's urgent new inquiry into the emerging El Obeid siege pattern is the next event to watch.

The benchmark's editors act on the proposed 23.8 of 100 downgrade, now reaffirmed for a fifth cycle. Release of detained rights defenders or reinstatement of the shuttered information-access authority would be conditions for an upgrade instead.
Coordinator-level review of Mali's score against Burkina Faso's for comparable military-government conduct. Roughly twenty-one days open with no set review date.
The same coordinator-level review, from Burkina Faso's side of the calibration question.
Coordinator-level review of whether Bolivia's score is calibrated consistently against Critical-band peers facing very different conditions. No review date has been set.
A documented mass-execution event or formal international atrocity finding is the next event that could move Iran's score toward the absolute floor.

A future priority-cluster assessment of the United States' own conduct toward Iran, including the resumed Hormuz blockade, is the next event that could move the United States' Critical-band composite.
First confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad.

Independent confirmation of a discrete own-conduct failure, such as documented aid diversion or denial of responder access, is the next event that would move the score toward a formal change from 18.0 of 100.
Independent verification of any renewed attempt at the collapsed prisoner exchange is the next event that would allow a scored assessment.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: A UN mission formally found Sudan's El Fasher killings bear 'distinct markers of genocide' and opened an inquiry into a similar pattern forming around El Obeid; will documented evidence from that inquiry constitute the first test of the benchmark's floor-confirmation category for an active, escalating atrocity?
